text.title: immersive-design-principles Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for immersive-design-principles. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Name the immersion level explicitly before designing any spatial experience. Never conflate passthrough and fully immersive — their comfort, interaction, and accessibility constraints are different.
rule.rationale: Agents conflate VR (occluded) and AR (passthrough); design decisions for one cause harm when applied to the other.

rule.statement: Place UI elements and interactive controls in the near zone (0.5-2 m), main content and interactive objects in the mid zone (2-10 m), and scenery in the far zone (10 m and beyond).
rule.rationale: Objects placed outside their appropriate depth zone cause eye strain (too near) or interaction failure (too far); depth zones match natural human vergence and accommodation ranges in XR.

rule.statement: Never head-lock UI elements. Use world-locked or body-locked panels at 1.5-3 m, eye level.
rule.rationale: Head-locked UI follows every head movement and causes immediate nausea; world-locked UI stays stable as the user looks around.

rule.statement: Implement realistic motion physics: gravity, momentum, inertia, collision feedback, and synchronized haptics. Couple every visual transition with an audio and haptic cue.
rule.rationale: Visual transitions without audio or haptic cues cause vestibular surprise — the brain expects multimodal confirmation of environment changes.

rule.statement: Prefer user-initiated transitions over automatic ones; use gradual fade in/out (not instant) with audio cues and visual mode indicators.
rule.rationale: Sudden mode changes without user agency are a primary source of disorientation and loss of presence.

rule.statement: Respond to physical space: adjust content to room size, warn about obstacles, reposition UI for comfort, and adapt to lighting conditions when in passthrough or blended modes.
rule.rationale: Ignoring physical context in blended and passthrough modes creates collision risk and UI overlap with physical objects.

rule.statement: Default every new XR experience to the highest-comfort settings; let users opt into lower comfort, never the opposite.
rule.rationale: A 5-10% nausea-prone subgroup will fail experiences that test fine on the rest; conservative defaults protect the most sensitive users without restricting others.

rule.statement: Always offer at least two locomotion methods (teleport plus smooth, or teleport plus seated). Pin teleport-as-default; smooth is opt-in with vignette on.
rule.rationale: Smooth locomotion without teleport excludes motion-sensitive users and wheelchair users; agents recommend smooth as the "modern" default and must be explicitly corrected.

rule.statement: Minimum 90 FPS per eye for VR; 60 FPS minimum for AR. Maximum 20 ms head-tracking latency and 50 ms controller latency. Drop visual fidelity before dropping below the FPS floor.
rule.rationale: Dropping below the performance floor causes simulator sickness regardless of all other design decisions; frame rate is non-negotiable.

rule.statement: Use human-scale for familiar objects (doors 2 m, tables 70-75 cm, chairs 45 cm). When scaling differently, make it obvious with a size reference (human for scale) and gradual transition.
rule.rationale: Unexpected scale breaks depth perception and comfortable interaction; scale ambiguity is disorienting.

rule.statement: Keep the floor/ground plane visible at all times with consistent appearance; maintain a stable horizon. These are the two primary grounding cues that reduce nausea.
rule.rationale: Loss of ground plane causes floating feeling and disorientation; an unstable horizon is the single fastest driver of vestibular mismatch.

rule.statement: Implement a guardian/chaperone system: visual boundary when approaching walls, haptic warning, audio alert option, pause on boundary crossing, easy re-centering.
rule.rationale: Physical collisions with real-world objects are a safety hazard; guardian systems are the standard mitigation across all major XR platforms.

rule.statement: Add visual captions in 3D space for all audio-cued events; captions must be size- and position-adjustable.
rule.rationale: Spatial-audio captions are commonly omitted; require a captions story for every voiced or audio-cued event in design review.

rule.statement: Seated mode must be fully functional: every interaction reachable while seated, all content viewable without standing or walking. Wheelchair-user parity is a hard requirement, not an afterthought.
rule.rationale: Seated mode is the most commonly omitted accessibility feature in room-scale VR design; agents skip it unless explicitly checked.

rule.statement: Treat WAI XAUR (W3C XR Accessibility User Requirements) as the requirements floor, not a nice-to-have. It covers disability cases that vendor design guidelines miss.
rule.rationale: Platform-vendor guidelines (Meta, Apple, Valve) are consensus documents that lag novel interaction patterns; XAUR is the authoritative accessibility requirements source for XR.
